item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Love this concept! One elephant in the room though: shouldn't Knossos on Crete be mentioned?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Could easily be that there is a future, and it's just inaccessible due to foundation activity.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Man, I sure hope the implication of the last line is simply that this is the last age in which temples are built that the labyrinth is able to manifest underneath… levels of religiosity within the general public is sharply on the slide after all.

Or maybe it's just that the Icelandic church never gets destroyed, or at least it's the last temple-like structure that ends up surviving on Earth for whatever reason.

Also, the idea of Ninjas using sharpened AOL CDs as durable yet easily hand-carved and non-clinking shuriken really tickles me for some reason :D

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Regardless, and although it's not really your fault, it seems to be a losing idea to include characters that won't render on the default install of a huge proportion of the site readership (Windows and Chrome are respectively the world's most popular OS and web browser, and the number of people who install additional character sets to either is likely so small as to be meaningless for SCP purposes). And Phoenican, if I'm understanding the context correctly, is unlikely to be included as a standard part of internet browser unicode any time soon, being a script that hasn't been in common use for millennia.

It may be a better idea either to find an alternative character that looks close enough to it to pass, but that is a default include in all common character sets, or just to render it as a small, inline image instead?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
POI-4242-𐤀: No. I cannot lead you to your future because this is the last age. I am sorry.

He stopped further commentary saying, "The cycle will continue, do not concern yourself with that."

These two lines (the second is from the supplement) certainly work together interestingly. I can draw a couple interpretations, the most obvious being that one of the two is wrong. But what if the cycle will continue past the last age? Is this merely the last age in a cycle, with the cycle being destined to start again soon?

item.title: Re: Some questions
item.content:encoded:
Communication is something that can be figured out when you've got speakers who are interested in communication with you. Someone will eventually learn the language and help with translation.

I specifically glossed over how time flows between periods for narrative reasons. Also, although I state that there is only one at a time, there's nothing that stops those periods of time to be near one another. Maybe it's just a year away.

It's even theoretically possible, if you travel deep enough between ages, to find times that are essentially contemporaries, and maybe there really is multiple 4242 instances at any given time, or maybe single instances in different timelines. But that, I'll leave to other authors to write.

Labyrinths are popular meditation tools across many different religions, and you'll often find one somewhere on many major places of worship. It might not be called out, but look around.

item.title: Some questions
item.content:encoded:
Can there only ever be one site with the labyrinth at a time?
How are they able to communicate with cultures whose language is long extinct? (e.g. the Neolithic culture in Scotland)
How does time pass? Is it contemporaneous? If you go to one time and spend a day there, is it a day later when you get back? If you wait in the present day is it however many days later in the past?
The article makes it sound like the Foundation can control where the labyrinth ends up. Is that the case? Or did they build the church around this site because it reverted there?
Also, why would there need to be a labyrinth for the public? Is that a thing in Lutheranism?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I assume they label them (at least in the present) so you know which time you're going to?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
So that's why there are so few ninjas on the wiki. The ones who do exist are really, really good at hiding.

This was a fantastic read. The list of items was a great note of humor in an otherwise somber and mysterious article. (90s AOL installation CDs sharpened into shurikens? at least SOMEONE found an actual use for the damn things!)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Gotcha. Thanks!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Maybe use an Arabic word for "grassland" or something similar instead?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: They travel to other labyrinth chambers, and those other chambers have labyrinths that lead back.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is awesome! I might have missed it, but how do people who use the labyrinths get back to the present?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Enjoyed this a lot. I tentatively agree that the ending line was predictable, but I was so absorbed in the article that I didn't predict it, so it still had a good impact on me. (Also, it's just what the traveler believes, right? Maybe we all End, or maybe there's a new beginning, something exciting to imagine and maybe even see. The cycle stuff from elsewhere in the article could indicate that…)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The wonderful build-up here makes the extraordinarily predictable ending all the more disappointing. You clearly signpost what is going on in the expectation of some twist or subversion, but then play straight something which is evident from the containment log, completely deflating the narrative tension. Strong -1; this is close to being great but really misses the mark in the final log.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Best one so far, although the labyrinth mechanics are a little hard to understand and it could do with a little more filler. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Is the last line a reference to SCP-2317?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Like a chunk of bricks. Very good. Very topical.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
It is a place now called Tassili n'Ajjer, mountains and valleys in the Sahara… Sahara desert, now, I suppose.

Minor nitpick here, but "Sahara" is Arabic for "desert," so this line sort of pulled me out of it. Great job otherwise. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
That's weird. I also switched my vote at that point, but for the exact opposite reason.

I thought the "last age" part was pretty well shown by the inability to access any future sites. The shinobi log in particular made it clear that they should be able to go forward, so the fact that they couldn't was an obvious sign. Spelling it out in the final log ruined the whole effect. It's not fridge horror if the author just tells you.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Dat ending. Definitely one of the better entries I’ve read thus far!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Very good article, definitely embodies the theme of the contest. My one complaint is that the ending is kind of abrupt, but that's not enough to prevent me from upvoting.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This is one of my favourite SCP-4000 contenders so far.
I'm pretty sure that where POI-4000 says:

We have found things, creatures, words that exist beyond the realms of men or gods.

You meant for him to say:

We have found things, creatures, worlds that exist beyond the realms of men or gods.

Unless he was talking about SCP-2979.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I really like the idea behind this. As it seems to me, every x000 SCP reveals something that somehow furthers the grand narrative of the Foundation across all individuals SCPs in an important way. Personally, i always have them in the back of my head when reading any other SCP. That's also what i feel is missing at PeppersGhost's submisson. But as spikebrennan already pointed out, the interview seems a bit dull and unauthentic, especially at the end. i think it would have been more interesting, if the Interview only hinted at the possibility that this is last age and that it would be the most logical conclusion for the reader to draw.
Compared to PeppersGhost's entry i like the idea of this SCP better, but I find the worldbuilding and overall execution of this SCP to be worse than the former. If you were to combine the both submissions it would make the perfect SCP-4000 for me. :)

Neutral Vote for me.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is good but not great. It addresses the theme remarkably well, but the ending was pretty predictable. Overall, it definitely deserves an upvote and a nice slot in the new series.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Well done.

The Foundation's use of the past's expertise in the containment of anomalies ties in nicely with the themes of the contest. I'm excited to see what additional entries the collab supplement adds, as well as crosslinks. Perhaps when the contest is over the Socratic Containment Procedures article and this could be linked via an addition, as they both touch onto similar themes.

Additionally, I found the final line of the interview to be particularly chilling. You a do a great job characterizing POI-4000-𐤀 and that build up and remorse in his final revelation, as well as the weight of his statement, is a great way of ending the article on a particularly strong note.

+1. Well done, WJS.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
While I liked the whole thematic of historical Foundation and Foundation-like organizations cooperating through time, overall I feel that the narrative buildup leading to the end of the article was a bit of a let-down.
The article already hints pretty heavily toward the middle that the present period does not have a future to go to, and so the expectation about the "big reveal" is that we are gonna get at least a nugget of information, something that hints at why/how; but we just get that line confirming something we already guessed and I personally found that a bit disappointing

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I was all ready to upvote this until the last interview, where the last line just came out of nowhere. The concept of this being the end of ages is a potentially great one which was never utilised earlier in the article and leaves the center of the article hollow. I'm all for subtlety and leaving unanswered questions, but this feels more like introducing an entirely new story and not going anywhere with it rather than a haunting closure to one.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I really appreciate that vote of confidence! I certainly had fun designing the ways this could exist throughout human history.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I don't know. To me, it rings more as 'the foundation will ultimately be successful in her efforts and no new scp-4000 site will need to appear' than as 'the foundation will fail in such a way that no new scp-4000 site can appear.' That's all interpretation, of course, so YMMV. In either case, a +1 from me.

And while you can't expand forward in time, there's plenty of room backward in time, between 10000 BCE and 2018.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: How exactly different people in the modern days travel to one point in time? I mean you pass the labyrinth and go to 44BCE, then you come back and a few days later you do the same, ending up at the same time in 44BCE.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The last line really hit me. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Great entry, especially with the punchline. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: That final line confirmed the suspicion I had when I saw the line about 0 future locations found. And yet chills still ran down my spine. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
IDK why reading the last line of the dialogue wetted my eyes.

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: There's some info about the Phoenician Unicode set on Wikipedia. Maybe it will help?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I use Chrome on Windows, yet somehow it's notoriously bad at having character sets. :(

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Yeah, it's done absolutely fantastically. At first it was one of the most wholesome 4000's, but at the end it turns bittersweet. Wonderful writing and characterization all around.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Is that really just supposed to be a square for that guy's designation?

It's a Phoenician aleph, but I can easily believe not all devices will render it as such.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Is that really just supposed to be a square for that guy's designation?

I love the sense of, for lack of a better term, temporal camaraderie this instills in me. It's like all the Foundations and Foundation analogues throughout time are one big neighborhood, and they gotta stick together for the sake of humanity. A little positivity is appreciated here. :)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The concept is good, but the final interview rings as inauthentic to me.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I think this is the entry that I want to win now. It uses nearly every idea of what the contest theme could mean in one concept.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I like it, just wish it ended in a more optimistic tone. Having no future, for me at least, takes out some of the charm. Also restricts how far this can expend.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Holy shit, this is good. Harrowing implications at the end, there. Does that final note certainly imply that there are no future ages, though, or could it also imply that SCP-4000 simply never moves location again in the future or that it gets destroyed in the near future?

Hearty +1 :)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I loved the last draft I read, but the dialogue changes to POI-4000-𐤀's log really sealed the deal. Excellent characterization and use of an individual character to convey the feel of the whole narrative.

item.title: Author Post
item.content:encoded:
Many thanks to Golden506, Riemann, A Random Day, and Cyantreuse for taking time to look this over! I know I'm forgetting at least one more person; just let me know.

I wanted to create something that everyone could build off, find new story ideas, and remind ourselves just how huge and wonderful this world is.

Photo Credits:

Hallgrímskirkja is by Örlygur Hnefill.
The finger labyrinth is by Hawobo.
Kasuga-taisha shrine is by FlyingToaster.
The Man from Africa is by the Collectie Stichting Nationaal Museum van Wereldculturen.
